units.text: Enter SLY and HOSTESS

units.speaker: SLY
units.text: I’ll pheeze you, in faith.

units.speaker: HOSTESS
units.text: A pair of stocks, you rogue!

units.speaker: SLY
units.text:
Y'are a baggage, the Slys are no rogues. Look in the
chronicles—we came in with Richard Conqueror. Therefore
paucas pallabris : let the world slide. Sessa!

units.speaker: HOSTESS
units.text: You will not pay for the glasses you have burst?

units.speaker: SLY
units.text:
No, not a denier. Go by, Saint Jeronimy. Go to thy cold
bed and warm thee.

units.speaker: HOSTESS
units.text: I know my remedy. I must go fetch the thirdborough.
Exit

units.speaker: SLY
units.text:
Third, or fourth, or fifth borough, I’ll answer him by
law.
I’ll not budge an inch, boy. Let him come, and kindly.
Falls asleep
Wind horns Enter a LORD from hunting, with his train

units.speaker: LORD
units.text:
Huntsman, I charge thee, tender well my hounds.
Breathe Merriman, the poor cur is embossed,
And couple Clowder with the deep-mouthed brach.
Saw’st thou not, boy, how Silver made it good
At the hedge corner, in the coldest fault?
I would not lose the dog for twenty pound.

units.speaker: FIRST HUNTSMAN
units.text:
Why, Belman is as good as he, my lord.
He cried upon it at the merest loss,
And twice today picked out the dullest scent.
Trust me, I take him for the better dog.

units.speaker: LORD
units.text:
Thou art a fool. If Echo were as fleet,
I would esteem him worth a dozen such.
But sup them well and look unto them all.
Tomorrow I intend to hunt again.

units.speaker: FIRST HUNTSMAN
units.text: I will, my lord.

units.speaker: LORD
units.text: What’s here? One dead, or drunk? See, doth he breathe?

units.speaker: SECOND HUNTSMAN
units.text:
He breathes, my lord. Were he not warmed with ale,
This were a bed but cold to sleep so soundly.

units.speaker: LORD
units.text:
O monstrous beast, how like a swine he lies!
Grim death, how foul and loathsome is thine image!
Sirs, I will practice on this drunken man.
What think you: if he were conveyed to bed,
Wrapped in sweet clothes, rings put upon his fingers,
A most delicious banquet by his bed,
And brave attendants near him when he wakes,
Would not the beggar then forget himself?

units.speaker: FIRST HUNTSMAN
units.text: Believe me, lord, I think he cannot choose.

units.speaker: SECOND HUNTSMAN
units.text: It would seem strange unto him when he waked.

units.speaker: LORD
units.text:
Even as a flatt'ring dream or worthless fancy.
Then take him up and manage well the jest.
Carry him gently to my fairest chamber
And hang it round with all my wanton pictures.
Balm his foul head in warm distilled waters
And burn sweet wood to make the lodging sweet.
Procure me music ready when he wakes,
To make a dulcet and a heavenly sound.
And if he chance to speak, be ready straight
And with a low submissive reverence
Say, “What is it your Honor will command?”
Let one attend him with a silver basin
Full of rose-water and bestrewed with flowers,
Another bear the ewer, the third a diaper,
And say, “Will ’t please your Lordship cool your
hands?”
Someone be ready with a costly suit
And ask him what apparel he will wear.
Another tell him of his hounds and horse,
And that his lady mourns at his disease.
Persuade him that he hath been lunatic,
And when he says he is, say that he dreams,
For he is nothing but a mighty lord.
This do, and do it kindly, gentle sirs.
It will be pastime passing excellent
If it be husbanded with modesty.

units.speaker: FIRST HUNTSMAN
units.text:
My lord, I warrant you we will play our part
As he shall think by our true diligence
He is no less than what we say he is.

units.speaker: LORD
units.text:
Take him up gently, and to bed with him,
And each one to his office when he wakes.
Some servants carry out SLY. Sound trumpets

units.speaker: LORD
units.text: Sirrah, go see what trumpet ’tis that sounds.
Exit Servingman

units.speaker: LORD
units.text:
Belike some noble gentleman that means,
Traveling some journey, to repose him here.
Enter SERVANT

units.speaker: LORD
units.text: How now! who is it?

units.speaker: SERVANT
units.text:
An’t please your Honor, players
That offer service to your Lordship.

units.speaker: LORD
units.text: Bid them come near.
Enter PLAYERS

units.speaker: LORD
units.text: Now, fellows, you are welcome.

units.speaker: PLAYERS
units.text: We thank your Honor.

units.speaker: LORD
units.text: Do you intend to stay with me tonight?

units.speaker: A PLAYER
units.text: So please your Lordship to accept our duty.

units.speaker: LORD
units.text:
With all my heart. This fellow I remember
Since once he played a farmer’s eldest son.
'Twas where you wooed the gentlewoman so well.
I have forgot your name, but sure that part
Was aptly fitted and naturally performed.

units.speaker: A PLAYER
units.text: I think ’twas Soto that your Honor means.

units.speaker: LORD
units.text:
'Tis very true. Thou didst it excellent.
Well, you are come to me in happy time,
The rather for I have some sport in hand
Wherein your cunning can assist me much.
There is a lord will hear you play tonight;
But I am doubtful of your modesties,
Lest over-eyeing of his odd behavior—
For yet his Honor never heard a play—
You break into some merry passion
And so offend him. For I tell you, sirs,
If you should smile, he grows impatient.

units.speaker: A PLAYER
units.text:
Fear not, my lord, we can contain ourselves
Were he the veriest antic in the world.

units.speaker: LORD
units.text:
Go, sirrah, take them to the buttery
And give them friendly welcome every one.
Let them want nothing that my house affords.
Exit one with the PLAYERS

units.speaker: LORD
units.text:
Sirrah, go you to Barthol’mew, my page,
And see him dressed in all suits like a lady.
That done, conduct him to the drunkard’s chamber
And call him “madam,” do him obeisance.
Tell him from me, as he will win my love,
He bear himself with honorable action,
Such as he hath observed in noble ladies
Unto their lords, by them accomplishèd.
Such duty to the drunkard let him do
With soft low tongue and lowly courtesy,
And say, “What is ’t your Honor will command,
Wherein your lady and your humble wife
May show her duty and make known her love?”
And then with kind embracements, tempting kisses,
And with declining head into his bosom,
Bid him shed tears, as being overjoyed
To see her noble lord restored to health,
Who for this seven years hath esteemed him
No better than a poor and loathsome beggar.
And if the boy have not a woman’s gift
To rain a shower of commanded tears,
An onion will do well for such a shift,
Which in a napkin being close conveyed
Shall in despite enforce a watery eye.
See this dispatched with all the haste thou canst:
Anon I’ll give thee more instructions.
Exit a servingman

units.speaker: LORD
units.text:
I know the boy will well usurp the grace,
Voice, gait, and action of a gentlewoman.
I long to hear him call the drunkard “husband,”
And how my men will stay themselves from laughter
When they do homage to this simple peasant.
I’ll in to counsel them. Haply my presence
May well abate the over-merry spleen
Which otherwise would grow into extremes.
Exeunt
Enter aloft SLY, the drunkard, with Attendants, some with apparel, others with basin and ewer and other appurtenances, and LORD dressed as an attendant.

units.speaker: SLY
units.text: For God’s sake, a pot of small ale.

units.speaker: FIRST SERVANT
units.text: Will ’t please your Lordship drink a cup of sack?

units.speaker: SECOND SERVANT
units.text: Will ’t please your Honor taste of these conserves?

units.speaker: THIRD SERVANT
units.text: What raiment will your Honor wear today?

units.speaker: SLY
units.text:
I am Christophero Sly. Call not me “Honor” nor
“Lordship.” I ne'er drank sack in my life. An if you
give me any conserves, give me conserves of beef. Ne'er
ask me what raiment I’ll wear, for I have no more
doublets than backs, no more stockings than legs, nor no
more shoes than feet, nay sometime more feet than
shoes, or such shoes as my toes look through the
over-leather.

units.speaker: LORD
units.text:
Heaven cease this idle humor in your Honor!
Oh, that a mighty man of such descent,
Of such possessions and so high esteem,
Should be infusèd with so foul a spirit!

units.speaker: SLY
units.text:
What, would you make me mad? Am not I Christopher Sly,
old Sly’s son of Burton Heath, by birth a peddler, by
education a cardmaker, by transmutation a bearherd, and
now by present profession a tinker? Ask Marian Hacket,
the fat alewife of Wincot, if she know me not! If she
say I am not fourteen pence on the score for sheer ale,
score me up for the lying’st knave in Christendom. What!
I am not bestraught! Here’s—

units.speaker: THIRD SERVANT
units.text: O, this it is that makes your lady mourn!

units.speaker: SECOND SERVANT
units.text: O, this is it that makes your servants droop!

units.speaker: LORD
units.text:
Hence comes it that your kindred shuns your house,
As beaten hence by your strange lunacy.
O noble lord, bethink thee of thy birth,
Call home thy ancient thoughts from banishment,
And banish hence these abject lowly dreams.
Look how thy servants do attend on thee,
Each in his office ready at thy beck.
Wilt thou have music? Hark! Apollo plays,
Music

units.speaker: LORD
units.text:
And twenty cagèd nightingales do sing:
Or wilt thou sleep? We’ll have thee to a couch
Softer and sweeter than the lustful bed
On purpose trimmed up for Semiramis.
Say thou wilt walk, we will bestrew the ground.
Or wilt thou ride? Thy horses shall be trapped,
Their harness studded all with gold and pearl.
Dost thou love hawking? Thou hast hawks will soar
Above the morning lark. Or wilt thou hunt?
Thy hounds shall make the welkin answer them
And fetch shrill echoes from the hollow earth.

units.speaker: FIRST SERVANT
units.text:
Say thou wilt course. Thy greyhounds are as swift
As breathed stags, ay, fleeter than the roe.

units.speaker: SECOND SERVANT
units.text:
Dost thou love pictures? We will fetch thee straight
Adonis painted by a running brook
And Cytherea all in sedges hid,
Which seem to move and wanton with her breath,
Even as the waving sedges play with wind.

units.speaker: LORD
units.text:
We’ll show thee Io as she was a maid
And how she was beguileèd and surprised,
As lively painted as the deed was done.

units.speaker: THIRD SERVANT
units.text:
Or Daphne roaming through a thorny wood,
Scratching her legs that one shall swear she bleeds,
And at that sight shall sad Apollo weep,
So workmanly the blood and tears are drawn.

units.speaker: LORD
units.text:
Thou art a lord, and nothing but a lord.
Thou hast a lady far more beautiful
Than any woman in this waning age.

units.speaker: FIRST SERVANT
units.text:
And till the tears that she hath shed for thee
Like envious floods o'errun her lovely face,
She was the fairest creature in the world—
And yet she is inferior to none.

units.speaker: SLY
units.text:
Am I a lord, and have I such a lady?
Or do I dream? Or have I dreamed till now?
I do not sleep: I see, I hear, I speak.
I smell sweet savors and I feel soft things.
Upon my life, I am a lord indeed
And not a tinker, nor Christopher Sly.
Well, bring our lady hither to our sight,
And once again, a pot o' the smallest ale.

units.speaker: SECOND SERVANT
units.text:
Will ’t please your Mightiness to wash your hands?
O, how we joy to see your wit restored!
O, that once more you knew but what you are!
These fifteen years you have been in a dream
Or, when you waked, so waked as if you slept.

units.speaker: SLY
units.text:
These fifteen years! By my fay, a goodly nap.
But did I never speak of all that time?

units.speaker: FIRST SERVANT
units.text:
O, yes, my lord, but very idle words.
For though you lay here in this goodly chamber,
Yet would you say ye were beaten out of door;
And rail upon the hostess of the house,
And say you would present her at the leet,
Because she brought stone jugs and no sealed quarts.
Sometimes you would call out for Cicely Hacket.

units.speaker: SLY
units.text: Ay, the woman’s maid of the house.

units.speaker: THIRD SERVANT
units.text:
Why, sir, you know no house nor no such maid,
Nor no such men as you have reckoned up,
As Stephen Sly and old John Naps of Greece,
And Peter Turph and Henry Pimpernell,
And twenty more such names and men as these,
Which never were, nor no man ever saw.

units.speaker: SLY
units.text: Now Lord be thanked for my good amends!

units.speaker: ALL
units.text: Amen.

units.speaker: SLY
units.text: I thank thee. Thou shalt not lose by it.
Enter the PAGE as a lady, with attendants

units.speaker: PAGE
units.text: How fares my noble lord?

units.speaker: SLY
units.text:
Marry, I fare well,
For here is cheer enough. Where is my wife?

units.speaker: PAGE
units.text: Here, noble lord. What is thy will with her?

units.speaker: SLY
units.text:
Are you my wife and will not call me “husband”?
My men should call me “lord.” I am your goodman.

units.speaker: PAGE
units.text:
My husband and my lord, my lord and husband,
I am your wife in all obedience.

units.speaker: SLY
units.text: I know it well.—What must I call her?

units.speaker: LORD
units.text: “Madam.”

units.speaker: SLY
units.text: “Alice Madam,” or “Joan Madam”?

units.speaker: LORD
units.text: “Madam,” and nothing else. So lords call ladies.

units.speaker: SLY
units.text:
Madam wife, they say that I have dreamed
And slept above some fifteen year or more.

units.speaker: PAGE
units.text:
Ay, and the time seems thirty unto me,
Being all this time abandoned from your bed.

units.speaker: SLY
units.text:
'Tis much.—Servants, leave me and her alone.
Madam, undress you and come now to bed.

units.speaker: PAGE
units.text:
Thrice noble lord, let me entreat of you
To pardon me yet for a night or two,
Or if not so, until the sun be set.
For your physicians have expressly charged,
In peril to incur your former malady,
That I should yet absent me from your bed.
I hope this reason stands for my excuse.

units.speaker: SLY
units.text:
Ay, it stands so that I may hardly tarry so long. But I
would be loath to fall into my dreams again. I will
therefore tarry in despite of the flesh and the blood.
Enter a MESSENGER

units.speaker: MESSENGER
units.text:
Your Honor’s players, hearing your amendment,
Are come to play a pleasant comedy,
For so your doctors hold it very meet,
Seeing too much sadness hath congealed your blood,
And melancholy is the nurse of frenzy.
Therefore they thought it good you hear a play
And frame your mind to mirth and merriment,
Which bars a thousand harms and lengthens life.

units.speaker: SLY
units.text:
Marry, I will. Let them play it. Is not a comonty a
Christmas gambold or a tumbling-trick?

units.speaker: PAGE
units.text: No, my good lord, it is more pleasing stuff.

units.speaker: SLY
units.text: What, household stuff?

units.speaker: PAGE
units.text: It is a kind of history.

units.speaker: SLY
units.text:
Well, we’ll see ’t. Come, madam wife, sit by my side
and let the world slip. We shall ne'er be younger.
Flourish. Enter LUCENTIO and his man TRANIO

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text:
Tranio, since for the great desire I had
To see fair Padua, nursery of arts,
I am arrived for fruitful Lombardy,
The pleasant garden of great Italy,
And by my father’s love and leave am armed
With his goodwill and thy good company.
My trusty servant, well approved in all,
Here let us breathe and haply institute
A course of learning and ingenious studies.
Pisa, renownèd for grave citizens,
Gave me my being and my father first,
A merchant of great traffic through the world,
Vincentio, come of the Bentivolii.
Vincentio’s son, brought up in Florence,
It shall become to serve all hopes conceived
To deck his fortune with his virtuous deeds.
And therefore, Tranio, for the time I study
Virtue, and that part of philosophy
Will I apply that treats of happiness
By virtue specially to be achieved.
Tell me thy mind, for I have Pisa left
And am to Padua come, as he that leaves
A shallow plash to plunge him in the deep
And with satiety seeks to quench his thirst.

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
Mi perdonato, gentle master mine.
I am in all affected as yourself,
Glad that you thus continue your resolve
To suck the sweets of sweet philosophy.
Only, good master, while we do admire
This virtue and this moral discipline,
Let’s be no stoics nor no stocks, I pray,
Or so devote to Aristotle’s checks
As Ovid be an outcast quite abjured.
Balk logic with acquaintance that you have,
And practice rhetoric in your common talk;
Music and poesy use to quicken you;
The mathematics and the metaphysics—
Fall to them as you find your stomach serves you.
No profit grows where is no pleasure ta'en.
In brief, sir, study what you most affect.

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text:
Gramercies, Tranio, well dost thou advise.
If, Biondello, thou wert come ashore,
We could at once put us in readiness
And take a lodging fit to entertain
Such friends as time in Padua shall beget.
But stay awhile. What company is this?

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text: Master, some show to welcome us to town.
LUCENTIO and TRANIO stand by
Enter BAPTISTA, KATHERINE, BIANCA, GREMIO, and HORTENSIO

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text:
Gentlemen, importune me no farther,
For how I firmly am resolved you know—
That is, not to bestow my youngest daughter
Before I have a husband for the elder.
If either of you both love Katherina,
Because I know you well and love you well
Leave shall you have to court her at your pleasure.

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text:
To cart her, rather. She’s too rough for me.—
There, there, Hortensio, will you any wife?

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text:
[To BAPTISTA] I pray you, sir, is it your will
To make a stale of me amongst these mates?

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text:
“Mates,” maid? how mean you that? No mates for you
Unless you were of gentler, milder mold.

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text:
I' faith, sir, you shall never need to fear.
I wis it is not halfway to her heart.
But if it were, doubt not her care should be
To comb your noddle with a three-legged stool
And paint your face and use you like a fool.

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text: From all such devils, good Lord, deliver us!

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text: And me too, good Lord!

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
[aside to LUCENTIO ]
Husht, master, here’s some good pastime toward.
That wench is stark mad or wonderful froward.

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text:
[aside to TRANIO ] But in the other’s silence do I see
Maid’s mild behavior and sobriety.
Peace, Tranio.

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
[aside to LUCENTIO ] Well said, master. Mum, and gaze
your fill.

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text:
[To GREMIO and HORTENSIO ]
Gentlemen, that I may soon make good
What I have said—Bianca, get you in,
And let it not displease thee, good Bianca,
For I will love thee ne'er the less, my girl.

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text:
A pretty peat! It is best
Put finger in the eye, an she knew why.

units.speaker: BIANCA
units.text:
Sister, content you in my discontent.—
Sir, to your pleasure humbly I subscribe.
My books and instruments shall be my company,
On them to look and practice by myself.

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text: Hark, Tranio! Thou may’st hear Minerva speak.

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text:
Signior Baptista, will you be so strange?
Sorry am I that our goodwill effects
Bianca’s grief.

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text:
Why will you mew her up,
Signior Baptista, for this fiend of hell
And make her bear the penance of her tongue?

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text:
Gentlemen, content ye. I am resolved.—
Go in, Bianca.
Exit BIANCA

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text:
And for I know she taketh most delight
In music, instruments, and poetry,
Schoolmasters will I keep within my house,
Fit to instruct her youth. If you, Hortensio,
Or, Signior Gremio, you know any such,
Prefer them hither, for to cunning men
I will be very kind, and liberal
To mine own children in good bringing up.
And so farewell.—Katherina, you may stay,
For I have more to commune with Bianca.
Exit

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text:
Why, and I trust I may go too, may I not? What, shall I
be appointed hours as though, belike, I knew not what
to take and what to leave, ha?
Exit

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text:
You may go to the devil’s dam! Your gifts are so good
here’s none will hold you. —Their love is not so great,
Hortensio, but we may blow our nails together and fast
it fairly out. Our cake’s dough on both sides. Farewell.
Yet for the love I bear my sweet Bianca, if I can by
any means light on a fit man to teach her that wherein
she delights, I will wish him to her father.

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text:
So will I, Signior Gremio. But a word, I pray. Though
the nature of our quarrel yet never brooked parle, know
now upon advice, it toucheth us both, that we may yet
again have access to our fair mistress and be happy
rivals in Bianca’s love, to labor and effect one thing
specially.

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text: What’s that, I pray?

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text: Marry, sir, to get a husband for her sister.

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text: A husband? A devil!

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text: I say a husband.

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text:
I say a devil. Think’st thou, Hortensio, though her
father be very rich, any man is so very a fool to be
married to hell?

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text:
Tush, Gremio. Though it pass your patience and mine to
endure her loud alarums, why, man, there be good fellows
in the world, an a man could light on them, would take
her with all faults, and money enough.

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text:
I cannot tell. But I had as lief take her dowry with
this condition: to be whipped at the high cross every
morning.

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text:
Faith, as you say, there’s small choice in rotten
apples. But come, since this bar in law makes us
friends, it shall be so far forth friendly maintained
till by helping Baptista’s eldest daughter to a husband
we set his youngest free for a husband, and then have to
’t afresh. Sweet Bianca! Happy man be his dole! He that
runs fastest gets the ring. How say you, Signior
Gremio?

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text:
I am agreed, and would I had given him the best horse
in Padua to begin his wooing that would thoroughly woo
her, wed her, and bed her, and rid the house of her!
Come on.
Exeunt GREMIO and HORTENSIO

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
I pray, sir, tell me, is it possible
That love should of a sudden take such hold?

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text:
O Tranio, till I found it to be true,
I never thought it possible or likely.
But see, while idly I stood looking on,
I found the effect of love in idleness
And now in plainness do confess to thee
That art to me as secret and as dear
As Anna to the Queen of Carthage was,
Tranio, I burn, I pine, I perish, Tranio,
If I achieve not this young modest girl.
Counsel me, Tranio, for I know thou canst.
Assist me, Tranio, for I know thou wilt.

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
Master, it is no time to chide you now.
Affection is not rated from the heart.
If love have touched you, naught remains but so:
Redime te captum quam queas minimo.

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text:
Gramercies, lad, go forward. This contents.
The rest will comfort, for thy counsel’s sound.

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
Master, you looked so longly on the maid,
Perhaps you marked not what’s the pith of all.

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text:
Oh yes, I saw sweet beauty in her face
Such as the daughter of Agenor had,
That made great Jove to humble him to her hand
When with his knees he kissed the Cretan strand.

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
Saw you no more? Marked you not how her sister
Began to scold and raise up such a storm
That mortal ears might hardly endure the din?

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text:
Tranio, I saw her coral lips to move
And with her breath she did perfume the air.
Sacred and sweet was all I saw in her.

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
[aside] Nay, then, ’tis time to stir him from his
trance.—
I pray, awake, sir! If you love the maid,
Bend thoughts and wits to achieve her. Thus it stands:
Her eldest sister is so curst and shrewd
That till the father rid his hands of her,
Master, your love must live a maid at home,
And therefore has he closely mewed her up,
Because she will not be annoyed with suitors.

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text:
Ah, Tranio, what a cruel father’s he!
But art thou not advised, he took some care
To get her cunning schoolmasters to instruct her?

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text: Ay, marry, am I, sir; and now ’tis plotted!

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text: I have it, Tranio!

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
Master, for my hand,
Both our inventions meet and jump in one.

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text: Tell me thine first.

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
You will be schoolmaster
And undertake the teaching of the maid:
That’s your device.

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text: It is. May it be done?

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
Not possible. For who shall bear your part
And be in Padua here Vincentio’s son,
Keep house and ply his book, welcome his friends,
Visit his countrymen and banquet them?

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text:
Basta, content thee, for I have it full.
We have not yet been seen in any house,
Nor can we be distinguished by our faces
For man or master. Then it follows thus:
Thou shalt be master, Tranio, in my stead,
Keep house and port and servants as I should.
I will some other be, some Florentine,
Some Neapolitan, or meaner man of Pisa.
'Tis hatched, and shall be so. Tranio, at once
Uncase thee. Take my colored hat and cloak.

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
So had you need.
In brief, sir, sith it your pleasure is,
And I am tied to be obedient—
For so your father charged me at our parting,
"Be serviceable to my son," quoth he,
Although I think 'twas in another sense—
I am content to be Lucentio
Because so well I love Lucentio.

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text:
Tranio, be so, because Lucentio loves,
And let me be a slave t'achieve that maid
Whose sudden sight hath thralled my wounded eye.
Enter BIONDELLO

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text: Here comes the rogue. Sirrah, where have you been?

units.speaker: BIONDELLO
units.text:
Where have I been? Nay, how now, where are you? Master,
has my fellow Tranio stolen your clothes? Or you stolen
his? Or both? Pray, what’s the news?

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text:
Sirrah, come hither: ’tis no time to jest,
And therefore frame your manners to the time.
Your fellow Tranio here, to save my life,
Puts my apparel and my countenance on,
And I for my escape have put on his;
For in a quarrel since I came ashore
I killed a man and fear I was descried.
Wait you on him, I charge you, as becomes,
While I make way from hence to save my life.
You understand me?

units.speaker: BIONDELLO
units.text: Aye, sir. [aside] Ne'er a whit.

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text:
And not a jot of “Tranio” in your mouth.
Tranio is changed into Lucentio.

units.speaker: BIONDELLO
units.text: The better for him. Would I were so too.

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
So could I, faith, boy, to have the next wish after,
That Lucentio indeed had Baptista’s youngest daughter.
But, sirrah, not for my sake, but your master’s, I
advise
You use your manners discreetly in all kind of
companies.
When I am alone, why then I am Tranio;
But in all places else, your master Lucentio.

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text:
Tranio, let’s go. One thing more rests, that thyself
execute,
To make one among these wooers. If thou ask me why,
Sufficeth my reasons are both good and weighty.
Exeunt

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text: The presenters above speak

units.speaker: FIRST SERVANT
units.text: My lord, you nod. You do not mind the play.

units.speaker: SLY
units.text:
Yes, by Saint Anne, do I. A good matter, surely. Comes
there any more of it?

units.speaker: PAGE
units.text: My lord, ’tis but begun.

units.speaker: SLY
units.text:
'Tis a very excellent piece of work, madam lady. Would
’twere done.
Enter PETRUCHIO and his man GRUMIO

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
Verona, for a while I take my leave,
To see my friends in Padua, but of all
My best belovèd and approvèd friend,
Hortensio. And I trow this is his house.
Here, sirrah Grumio. Knock, I say.

units.speaker: GRUMIO
units.text:
Knock, sir? Whom should I knock? Is there any man has
rebused your Worship?

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: Villain, I say, knock me here soundly.

units.speaker: GRUMIO
units.text:
Knock you here, sir? Why, sir, what am I, sir, that I
should knock you here, sir?

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
Villain, I say, knock me at this gate
And rap me well, or I’ll knock your knave’s pate.

units.speaker: GRUMIO
units.text:
My master is grown quarrelsome. I should knock you
first,
And then I know after who comes by the worst.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
Will it not be?
Faith, sirrah, an you’ll not knock, I’ll ring it.
I’ll try how you can sol, fa, and sing it.

units.speaker: GRUMIO
units.text: Help, mistress, help! My master is mad.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: Now knock when I bid you, sirrah villain.
Enter HORTENSIO

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text:
How now, what’s the matter? My old friend Grumio and
my good friend Petruchio? How do you all at Verona?

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
Signior Hortensio, come you to part the fray?
Con tutto il cuore, ben trovato, may I say.

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text:
Alla nostra casa ben venuto, molto honorato signor mio
Petruchio.—Rise, Grumio, rise. We will compound this
quarrel.

units.speaker: GRUMIO
units.text:
Nay, ’tis no matter, sir, what he 'leges in Latin. If
this be not a lawful case for me to leave his service
—look you, sir: he bid me knock him and rap him soundly,
sir. Well, was it fit for a servant to use his master
so, being perhaps, for aught I see, two-and-thirty, a
pip out?
Whom, would to God, I had well knocked at first,
Then had not Grumio come by the worst.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
A senseless villain, good Hortensio.
I bade the rascal knock upon your gate
And could not get him for my heart to do it.

units.speaker: GRUMIO
units.text:
Knock at the gate? O heavens! Spake you not these words
plain: “Sirrah, knock me here, rap me here, knock me
well, and knock me soundly”? And come you now with
“knocking at the gate”?

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: Sirrah, begone or talk not, I advise you.

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text:
Petruchio, patience. I am Grumio’s pledge.
Why, this' a heavy chance ’twixt him and you,
Your ancient, trusty, pleasant servant Grumio.
And tell me now, sweet friend, what happy gale
Blows you to Padua here from old Verona?

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
Such wind as scatters young men through the world
To seek their fortunes farther than at home,
Where small experience grows. But in a few,
Signior Hortensio, thus it stands with me:
Antonio, my father, is deceased,
And I have thrust myself into this maze,
Happily to wive and thrive as best I may.
Crowns in my purse I have and goods at home,
And so am come abroad to see the world.

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text:
Petruchio, shall I then come roundly to thee
And wish thee to a shrewd, ill-favored wife?
Thou’dst thank me but a little for my counsel;
And yet I’ll promise thee she shall be rich,
And very rich. But thou'rt too much my friend,
And I’ll not wish thee to her.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
Signior Hortensio, ’twixt such friends as we
Few words suffice. And therefore, if thou know
One rich enough to be Petruchio’s wife,
As wealth is burden of my wooing dance,
Be she as foul as was Florentius' love,
As old as Sibyl and as curst and shrewd
As Socrates' Xanthippe, or a worse,
She moves me not, or not removes at least
Affection’s edge in me, were she as rough
As are the swelling Adriatic seas.
I come to wive it wealthily in Padua;
If wealthily, then happily in Padua.

units.speaker: GRUMIO
units.text:
[To HORTENSIO] Nay, look you, sir, he tells you flatly
what his mind is. Why, give him gold enough and marry
him to a puppet or an aglet-baby, or an old trot with
ne'er a tooth in her head, though she have as many
diseases as two-and-fifty horses. Why, nothing comes
amiss, so money comes withal.

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text:
Petruchio, since we are stepped thus far in,
I will continue that I broached in jest.
I can, Petruchio, help thee to a wife
With wealth enough, and young and beauteous,
Brought up as best becomes a gentlewoman.
Her only fault, and that is faults enough,
Is that she is intolerable curst,
And shrewd and froward, so beyond all measure
That, were my state far worser than it is,
I would not wed her for a mine of gold.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
Hortensio, peace. Thou know’st not gold’s effect.
Tell me her father’s name, and ’tis enough;
For I will board her, though she chide as loud
As thunder when the clouds in autumn crack.

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text:
Her father is Baptista Minola,
An affable and courteous gentleman.
Her name is Katherina Minola,
Renowned in Padua for her scolding tongue.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
I know her father, though I know not her,
And he knew my deceasèd father well.
I will not sleep, Hortensio, till I see her,
And therefore let me be thus bold with you
To give you over at this first encounter,
Unless you will accompany me thither.

units.speaker: GRUMIO
units.text:
[To HORTENSIO] I pray you, sir, let him go while the
humor lasts. O' my word, an she knew him as well as I
do, she would think scolding would do little good upon
him. She may perhaps call him half a score knaves or so.
Why, that’s nothing; an he begin once, he’ll rail in
his rope tricks. I’ll tell you what sir: an she stand
him but a little, he will throw a figure in her face and
so disfigure her with it that she shall have no more
eyes to see withal than a cat. You know him not, sir.

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text:
Tarry, Petruchio, I must go with thee,
For in Baptista’s keep my treasure is.
He hath the jewel of my life in hold,
His youngest daughter, beautiful Bianca,
And her withholds from me and other more,
Suitors to her and rivals in my love,
Supposing it a thing impossible,
For those defects I have before rehearsed,
That ever Katherina will be wooed.
Therefore this order hath Baptista ta'en,
That none shall have access unto Bianca
Till Katherine the curst have got a husband.

units.speaker: GRUMIO
units.text:
“Katherine the curst!”
A title for a maid of all titles the worst.

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text:
Now shall my friend Petruchio do me grace,
And offer me disguised in sober robes
To old Baptista as a schoolmaster
Well seen in music, to instruct Bianca,
That so I may, by this device at least,
Have leave and leisure to make love to her
And, unsuspected, court her by herself.

units.speaker: GRUMIO
units.text:
Here’s no knavery! See, to beguile the old folks, how
the young folks lay their heads together!
Enter GREMIO and LUCENTIO disguised as CAMBIO

units.speaker: GRUMIO
units.text: Master, master, look about you. Who goes there, ha?

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text:
Peace, Grumio. It is the rival of my love.
Petruchio, stand by a while.

units.speaker: GRUMIO
units.text: [aside] A proper stripling, and an amorous.

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text:
[To LUCENTIO] O, very well, I have perused the note.
Hark you, sir: I’ll have them very fairly bound,
All books of love. See that at any hand,
And see you read no other lectures to her.
You understand me. Over and beside
Signior Baptista’s liberality,
I’ll mend it with a largess. Take your paper too.
And let me have them very well perfum’d
For she is sweeter than perfume itself
To whom they go to. What will you read to her?

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text:
[as CAMBIO] Whate'er I read to her, I’ll plead for you
As for my patron, stand you so assured,
As firmly as yourself were still in place,
Yea, and perhaps with more successful words
Than you, unless you were a scholar, sir.

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text: O this learning, what a thing it is!

units.speaker: GRUMIO
units.text: [aside] O this woodcock, what an ass it is!

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: [aside] Peace, sirrah.

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text: [aside] Grumio, mum! God save you, Signior Gremio.

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text:
And you are well met, Signior Hortensio.
Trow you whither I am going? To Baptista Minola.
I promised to enquire carefully
About a schoolmaster for the fair Bianca,
And by good fortune I have lighted well
On this young man, for learning and behavior
Fit for her turn, well read in poetry
And other books—good ones, I warrant ye.

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text:
'Tis well. And I have met a gentleman
Hath promised me to help me to another,
A fine musician to instruct our mistress.
So shall I no whit be behind in duty
To fair Bianca, so beloved of me.

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text: Beloved of me, and that my deeds shall prove.

units.speaker: GRUMIO
units.text: [aside] And that his bags shall prove.

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text:
Gremio, ’tis now no time to vent our love.
Listen to me, and if you speak me fair,
I’ll tell you news indifferent good for either.
[presenting PETRUCHIO ]
Here is a gentleman whom by chance I met,
Upon agreement from us to his liking,
Will undertake to woo curst Katherine,
Yea, and to marry her, if her dowry please.

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text:
So said, so done, is well.
Hortensio, have you told him all her faults?

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
I know she is an irksome brawling scold.
If that be all, masters, I hear no harm.

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text: No? Say’st me so, friend? What countryman?

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
Born in Verona, old Antonio’s son.
My father dead, my fortune lives for me.
And I do hope good days and long to see.

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text:
O sir, such a life with such a wife were strange!
But if you have a stomach, to ’t, i' God’s name:
You shall have me assisting you in all.
But will you woo this wildcat?

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: Will I live?

units.speaker: GRUMIO
units.text: Will he woo her? Ay, or I’ll hang her.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
Why came I hither but to that intent?
Think you a little din can daunt mine ears?
Have I not in my time heard lions roar?
Have I not heard the sea, puffed up with winds,
Rage like an angry boar chafèd with sweat?
Have I not heard great ordnance in the field,
And heaven’s artillery thunder in the skies?
Have I not in a pitchèd battle heard
Loud 'larums, neighing steeds, and trumpets' clang?
And do you tell me of a woman’s tongue
That gives not half so great a blow to hear
As will a chestnut in a farmer’s fire?
Tush, tush! Fear boys with bugs.

units.speaker: GRUMIO
units.text: For he fears none.

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text:
Hortensio, hark.
This gentleman is happily arrived,
My mind presumes, for his own good and ours.

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text:
I promised we would be contributors
And bear his charge of wooing, whatsoe'er.

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text: And so we will, provided that he win her.

units.speaker: GRUMIO
units.text: I would I were as sure of a good dinner.
Enter TRANIO brave and BIONDELLO

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
[as LUCENTIO] Gentlemen, God save you. If I may be
bold,
Tell me, I beseech you, which is the readiest way
To the house of Signior Baptista Minola?

units.speaker: BIONDELLO
units.text: He that has the two fair daughters—is ’t he you mean?

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text: [as LUCENTIO] Even he, Biondello.

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text: Hark you, sir, you mean not her to—

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
[as LUCENTIO]
Perhaps him and her, sir. What have you to do?

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: Not her that chides, sir, at any hand, I pray.

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
[as LUCENTIO] I love no chiders, sir. Biondello, let’s
away.

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text: [aside] Well begun, Tranio.

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text:
Sir, a word ere you go.
Are you a suitor to the maid you talk of, yea or no?

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text: An if I be, sir, is it any offense?

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text: No, if without more words you will get you hence.

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
[as LUCENTIO] Why, sir, I pray, are not the streets as
free For me as for you?

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text: But so is not she.

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text: For what reason, I beseech you?

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text:
For this reason, if you’ll know:
That she’s the choice love of Signior Gremio.

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text: That she’s the chosen of Signior Hortensio.

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
Softly, my masters. If you be gentlemen,
Do me this right: hear me with patience.
Baptista is a noble gentleman,
To whom my father is not all unknown,
And were his daughter fairer than she is,
She may more suitors have, and me for one.
Fair Leda’s daughter had a thousand wooers;
Then well one more may fair Bianca have.
And so she shall. Lucentio shall make one,
Though Paris came in hope to speed alone.

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text: What! This gentleman will out-talk us all.

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text:
[as CAMBIO] Sir, give him head; I know he’ll prove a
jade.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: Hortensio, to what end are all these words?

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text:
[To TRANIO] Sir, let me be so bold as ask you,
Did you yet ever see Baptista’s daughter?

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
[as LUCENTIO] No, sir, but hear I do that he hath two,
The one as famous for a scolding tongue
As is the other for beauteous modesty.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: Sir, sir, the first’s for me; let her go by.

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text:
Yea, leave that labor to great Hercules,
And let it be more than Alcides' twelve.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
[To TRANIO] Sir, understand you this of me, in sooth:
The youngest daughter, whom you hearken for,
Her father keeps from all access of suitors
And will not promise her to any man
Until the elder sister first be wed.
The younger then is free, and not before.

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
[as LUCENTIO] If it be so, sir, that you are the man
Must stead us all, and me amongst the rest,
And if you break the ice and do this feat,
Achieve the elder, set the younger free
For our access, whose hap shall be to have her
Will not so graceless be to be ingrate.

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text:
Sir, you say well, and well you do conceive.
And since you do profess to be a suitor,
You must, as we do, gratify this gentleman,
To whom we all rest generally beholding.

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
[as LUCENTIO] Sir, I shall not be slack; in sign
whereof,
Please ye we may contrive this afternoon
And quaff carouses to our mistress' health
And do as adversaries do in law,
Strive mightily, but eat and drink as friends.

units.speaker: GRUMIO AND BIONDELLO
units.text: O excellent motion! Fellows, let’s be gone.

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text:
The motion’s good indeed and be it so.—
Petruchio, I shall be your ben venuto.
Exeunt
Enter KATHERINE and BIANCA, her hands bound

units.speaker: BIANCA
units.text:
Good sister, wrong me not nor wrong yourself,
To make a bondmaid and a slave of me.
That I disdain. But for these other goods—
Unbind my hands, I’ll pull them off myself,
Yea, all my raiment to my petticoat,
Or what you will command me will I do,
So well I know my duty to my elders.

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text:
Of all thy suitors here I charge thee tell
Whom thou lovest best. See thou dissemble not.

units.speaker: BIANCA
units.text:
Believe me, sister, of all the men alive
I never yet beheld that special face
Which I could fancy more than any other.

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text: Minion, thou liest. Is ’t not Hortensio?

units.speaker: BIANCA
units.text:
If you affect him, sister, here I swear
I’ll plead for you myself, but you shall have him.

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text:
Oh, then belike you fancy riches more.
You will have Gremio to keep you fair.

units.speaker: BIANCA
units.text:
Is it for him you do envy me so?
Nay, then you jest, and now I well perceive
You have but jested with me all this while.
I prithee, sister Kate, untie my hands.

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text: If that be jest, then all the rest was so.
Enter BAPTISTA

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text:
Why, how now, dame! whence grows this insolence?—
Bianca, stand aside.—Poor girl, she weeps!
[To BIANCA] Go ply thy needle; meddle not with her.
[To KATHERINE] For shame, thou hilding of a devilish
spirit!
Why dost thou wrong her that did ne'er wrong thee?
When did she cross thee with a bitter word?

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text: Her silence flouts me, and I’ll be revenged.

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text: What, in my sight?—Bianca, get thee in.
Exit BIANCA

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text:
What, will you not suffer me? Nay, now I see
She is your treasure, she must have a husband,
I must dance barefoot on her wedding day
And, for your love to her, lead apes in hell.
Talk not to me. I will go sit and weep
Till I can find occasion of revenge.
Exit

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text:
Was ever gentleman thus grieved as I?
But who comes here?
Enter GREMIO, LUCENTIO in the habit of a mean man; PETRUCHIO, with HORTENSIO as a musician; and TRANIO, with BIONDELLO bearing a lute and books

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text: Good morrow, neighbor Baptista.

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text: Good morrow, neighbor Gremio.—God save you, gentlemen!

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
And you, good sir. Pray, have you not a daughter
Called Katherina, fair and virtuous?

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text: I have a daughter, sir, called Katherina.

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text: [To PETRUCHIO] You are too blunt. Go to it orderly.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
You wrong me, Signior Gremio. Give me leave.—
I am a gentleman of Verona, sir,
That hearing of her beauty and her wit,
Her affability and bashful modesty,
Her wondrous qualities and mild behavior,
Am bold to show myself a forward guest
Within your house, to make mine eye the witness
Of that report which I so oft have heard.
And, for an entrance to my entertainment,
I do present you with a man of mine,
[presenting HORTENSIO , disguised as LITIO ]
Cunning in music and the mathematics,
To instruct her fully in those sciences,
Whereof I know she is not ignorant.
Accept of him, or else you do me wrong.
His name is Litio, born in Mantua.

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text:
You’re welcome, sir, and he for your good sake.
But for my daughter Katherine, this I know,
She is not for your turn, the more my grief.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
I see you do not mean to part with her,
Or else you like not of my company.

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text:
Mistake me not. I speak but as I find.
Whence are you, sir? What may I call your name?

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
Petruchio is my name, Antonio’s son,
A man well known throughout all Italy.

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text: I know him well. You are welcome for his sake.

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text:
Saving your tale, Petruchio, I pray
Let us that are poor petitioners speak too.
Bacare, you are marvelous forward.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: Oh, pardon me, Signior Gremio, I would fain be doing.

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text:
I doubt it not, sir, but you will curse your wooing.—
[To BAPTISTA ] Neighbor, this is a gift very grateful,
I am sure of it. To express the like kindness, myself,
that have been more kindly beholding to you than any,
freely give unto you this young scholar [presenting
LUCENTIO , disguised as CAMBIO ] that hath been long
studying at Rheims, as cunning in Greek, Latin, and
other languages as the other in music and mathematics.
His name is Cambio. Pray accept his service.

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text:
A thousand thanks, Signior Gremio. Welcome, good
Cambio. [To TRANIO as LUCENTIO ] But, gentle sir,
methinks you walk like a stranger. May I be so bold to
know the cause of your coming?

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
[as LUCENTIO] Pardon me, sir, the boldness is mine
own,
That being a stranger in this city here
Do make myself a suitor to your daughter,
Unto Bianca, fair and virtuous.
Nor is your firm resolve unknown to me,
In the preferment of the eldest sister.
This liberty is all that I request,
That, upon knowledge of my parentage,
I may have welcome ’mongst the rest that woo
And free access and favor as the rest.
And toward the education of your daughters,
I here bestow a simple instrument
And this small packet of Greek and Latin books.
[BIONDELLO b rings the gifts forward]
If you accept them, then their worth is great.

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text: Lucentio is your name. Of whence, I pray?

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text: [as LUCENTIO] Of Pisa, sir, son to Vincentio.

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text:
A mighty man of Pisa. By report
I know him well. You are very welcome, sir.
[To HORTENSIO as LITIO ] Take you the lute,
[To LUCENTIO as CAMBIO ] and you the set of books.
You shall go see your pupils presently.
Holla, within!
Enter a Servant

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text:
Sirrah, lead these gentlemen
To my daughters, and tell them both
These are their tutors. Bid them use them well.
Exit Servant with LUCENTIO and HORTENSIO, BIONDELLO following

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text:
We will go walk a little in the orchard,
And then to dinner. You are passing welcome,
And so I pray you all to think yourselves.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
Signior Baptista, my business asketh haste,
And every day I cannot come to woo.
You knew my father well, and in him me,
Left solely heir to all his lands and goods,
Which I have bettered rather than decreased.
Then tell me, if I get your daughter’s love,
What dowry shall I have with her to wife?

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text:
After my death, the one half of my lands,
And, in possession, twenty thousand crowns.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
And, for that dowry, I’ll assure her of
Her widowhood, be it that she survive me,
In all my lands and leases whatsoever.
Let specialties be therefore drawn between us,
That covenants may be kept on either hand.

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text:
Ay, when the special thing is well obtained,
That is, her love, for that is all in all.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
Why, that is nothing. For I tell you, father,
I am as peremptory as she proud-minded;
And where two raging fires meet together,
They do consume the thing that feeds their fury.
Though little fire grows great with little wind,
Yet extreme gusts will blow out fire and all.
So I to her and so she yields to me,
For I am rough and woo not like a babe.

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text:
Well mayst thou woo, and happy be thy speed.
But be thou armed for some unhappy words.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
Ay, to the proof, as mountains are for winds,
That shakes not, though they blow perpetually.
Enter HORTENSIO as LITIO, with his head broke

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text: How now, my friend, why dost thou look so pale?

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text: [as LITIO] For fear, I promise you, if I look pale.

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text: What, will my daughter prove a good musician?

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text:
I think she’ll sooner prove a soldier.
Iron may hold with her, but never lutes.

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text: Why, then thou canst not break her to the lute?

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text:
Why, no, for she hath broke the lute to me.
I did but tell her she mistook her frets,
And bowed her hand to teach her fingering,
When, with a most impatient devilish spirit,
“'Frets' call you these?” quoth she. “I’ll fume with
them!”
And with that word she struck me on the head,
And through the instrument my pate made way,
And there I stood amazèd for a while
As on a pillory, looking through the lute,
While she did call me “rascal fiddler”
And “twangling Jack”; with twenty such vile terms,
As had she studied to misuse me so.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
Now, by the world, it is a lusty wench.
I love her ten times more than e'er I did.
O, how I long to have some chat with her!

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text:
[To HORTENSIO as LITIO]
Well, go with me and be not so discomfited.
Proceed in practice with my younger daughter.
She’s apt to learn and thankful for good turns.
Signior Petruchio, will you go with us,
Or shall I send my daughter Kate to you?

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: I pray you do.
Exeunt all but PETRUCHIO

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
I’ll attend her here
And woo her with some spirit when she comes.
Say that she rail; why then I’ll tell her plain
She sings as sweetly as a nightingale.
Say that she frown; I’ll say she looks as clear
As morning roses newly washed with dew.
Say she be mute and will not speak a word;
Then I’ll commend her volubility,
And say she uttereth piercing eloquence.
If she do bid me pack, I’ll give her thanks,
As though she bid me stay by her a week.
If she deny to wed, I’ll crave the day
When I shall ask the banns and when be marrièd.
But here she comes—and now, Petruchio, speak.
Enter KATHERINE

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: Good morrow, Kate—for that’s your name, I hear.

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text:
Well have you heard, but something hard of hearing.
They call me Katherine that do talk of me.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
You lie, in faith, for you are called plain Kate,
And bonny Kate, and sometimes Kate the curst,
But Kate, the prettiest Kate in Christendom,
Kate of Kate Hall, my super-dainty Kate—
For dainties are all Kates —and therefore, Kate,
Take this of me, Kate of my consolation:
Hearing thy mildness praised in every town,
Thy virtues spoke of, and thy beauty sounded—
Yet not so deeply as to thee belongs—
Myself am moved to woo thee for my wife.

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text:
“Moved,” in good time. Let him that moved you hither
Remove you hence. I knew you at the first
You were a moveable.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: Why, what’s a moveable?

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text: A joint stool.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: Thou hast hit it. Come, sit on me.

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text: Asses are made to bear, and so are you.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: Women are made to bear, and so are you.

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text: No such jade as you, if me you mean.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
Alas, good Kate, I will not burden thee,
For knowing thee to be but young and light—

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text:
Too light for such a swain as you to catch,
And yet as heavy as my weight should be.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: “Should be”—should buzz!

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text: Well ta'en, and like a buzzard.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: O slow-winged turtle, shall a buzzard take thee?

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text: Ay, for a turtle, as he takes a buzzard.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: Come, come, you wasp. I' faith, you are too angry.

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text: If I be waspish, best beware my sting.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: My remedy is then to pluck it out.

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text: Ay, if the fool could find it where it lies.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
Who knows not where a wasp does wear his sting?
In his tail.

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text: In his tongue.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: Whose tongue?

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text: Yours, if you talk of tales. And so farewell.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
What, with my tongue in your tail? Nay, come again,
Good Kate. I am a gentleman.

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text: That I’ll try.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: I swear I’ll cuff you if you strike again.

units.speaker: KATHERINE
PETRUCHIO
units.text: A herald, Kate? Oh, put me in thy books!

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text: What is your crest? A coxcomb?

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: A combless cock, so Kate will be my hen.

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text: No cock of mine. You crow too like a craven.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: Nay, come, Kate, come. You must not look so sour.

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text: It is my fashion, when I see a crab.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: Why, here’s no crab, and therefore look not sour.

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text: There is, there is.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: Then show it me.

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text: Had I a glass, I would.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: What, you mean my face?

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text: Well aimed of such a young one.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: Now, by Saint George, I am too young for you.

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text: Yet you are withered.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: 'Tis with cares.

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text: I care not.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: Nay, hear you, Kate: in sooth you ’scape not so.

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text: I chafe you, if I tarry. Let me go.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
No, not a whit. I find you passing gentle.
'Twas told me you were rough and coy and sullen,
And now I find report a very liar.
For thou are pleasant, gamesome, passing courteous,
But slow in speech, yet sweet as springtime flowers.
Thou canst not frown, thou canst not look askance,
Nor bite the lip as angry wenches will,
Nor hast thou pleasure to be cross in talk.
But thou with mildness entertain’st thy wooers,
With gentle conference, soft and affable.
Why does the world report that Kate doth limp?
O slanderous world! Kate like the hazel-twig
As hazel nuts, and sweeter than the kernels.
Oh, let me see thee walk! Thou dost not halt.

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text: Go, fool, and whom thou keep’st command.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
Did ever Dian so become a grove
As Kate this chamber with her princely gait?
Oh, be thou Dian, and let her be Kate,
And then let Kate be chaste and Dian sportful.

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text: Where did you study all this goodly speech?

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: It is extempore, from my mother wit.

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text: A witty mother! Witless else her son.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: Am I not wise?

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text: Yes, keep you warm.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
Marry, so I mean, sweet Katherine, in thy bed.
And therefore, setting all this chat aside,
Thus in plain terms: your father hath consented
That you shall be my wife, your dowry 'greed on,
And, will you, nill you, I will marry you.
Now, Kate, I am a husband for your turn,
For, by this light, whereby I see thy beauty,
Thy beauty that doth make me like thee well,
Thou must be married to no man but me.
For I am he am born to tame you, Kate,
And bring you from a wild Kate to a Kate
Conformable as other household Kates.
Enter BAPTISTA, GREMIO, and TRANIO

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
Here comes your father. Never make denial.
I must and will have Katherine to my wife.

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text: Now, Signior Petruchio, how speed you with my daughter?

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
How but well, sir? How but well?
It were impossible I should speed amiss.

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text: Why, how now, daughter Katherine? In your dumps?

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text:
Call you me daughter? Now, I promise you
You have showed a tender fatherly regard
To wish me wed to one half lunatic,
A madcup ruffian and a swearing Jack,
That thinks with oaths to face the matter out.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
Father, ’tis thus: yourself and all the world
That talked of her have talked amiss of her.
If she be curst, it is for policy,
For she’s not froward, but modest as the dove.
She is not hot, but temperate as the morn.
For patience she will prove a second Grissel,
And Roman Lucrece for her chastity.
And to conclude, we have 'greed so well together,
That upon Sunday is the wedding day.

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text: I’ll see thee hanged on Sunday first.

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text: Hark, Petruchio: she says she’ll see thee hanged first.

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text: Is this your speeding? Nay, then, good night our part.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
Be patient, gentlemen. I choose her for myself.
If she and I be pleased, what’s that to you?
'Tis bargained ’twixt us twain, being alone,
That she shall still be curst in company.
I tell you, ’tis incredible to believe
How much she loves me. O, the kindest Kate!
She hung about my neck, and kiss on kiss
She vied so fast, protesting oath on oath,
That in a twink she won me to her love.
O, you are novices! 'Tis a world to see,
How tame, when men and women are alone,
A meacock wretch can make the curstest shrew.—
Give me thy hand, Kate. I will unto Venice
To buy apparel 'gainst the wedding day.
Provide the feast, father, and bid the guests.
I will be sure my Katherine shall be fine.

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text:
I know not what to say, but give me your hands.
God send you joy, Petruchio. 'Tis a match.

units.speaker: GREMIO AND TRANIO
units.text: Amen, say we. We will be witnesses.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
Father, and wife, and gentlemen, adieu.
I will to Venice. Sunday comes apace.
We will have rings, and things, and fine array,
And kiss me, Kate. We will be married o' Sunday.
Exeunt PETRUCHIO and KATHERINE severally

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text: Was ever match clapped up so suddenly?

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text:
Faith, gentlemen, now I play a merchant’s part,
And venture madly on a desperate mart.

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
[as LUCENTIO] 'Twas a commodity lay fretting by you.
'Twill bring you gain or perish on the seas.

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text: The gain I seek is quiet in the match.

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text:
No doubt but he hath got a quiet catch.
But now, Baptista, to your younger daughter.
Now is the day we long have lookèd for.
I am your neighbor, and was suitor first.

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
[as LUCENTIO] And I am one that love Bianca more
Than words can witness or your thoughts can guess.

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text: Youngling, thou canst not love so dear as I.

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
[as LUCENTIO]
Graybeard, thy love doth freeze.

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text:
But thine doth fry.
Skipper, stand back. 'Tis age that nourisheth.

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
[as LUCENTIO] But youth in ladies' eyes that
flourisheth.

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text:
Content you, gentlemen. I will compound this strife.
'Tis deeds must win the prize, and he of both
That can assure my daughter greatest dower
Shall have my Bianca’s love.
Say, Signior Gremio, what can you assure her?

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text:
First, as you know, my house within the city
Is richly furnishèd with plate and gold,
Basins and ewers to lave her dainty hands;
My hangings all of Tyrian tapestry,
In ivory coffers I have stuffed my crowns,
In cypress chests my arras counterpoints,
Costly apparel, tents, and canopies,
Fine linen, Turkey cushions bossed with pearl,
Valance of Venice gold in needlework,
Pewter and brass, and all things that belong
To house or housekeeping. Then, at my farm
I have a hundred milch-kine to the pail,
Six score fat oxen standing in my stalls,
And all things answerable to this portion.
Myself am struck in years, I must confess,
And if I die tomorrow this is hers,
If whilst I live she will be only mine.

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
(as LUCENTIO]
That “only” came well in. [To BAPTISTA] Sir, list to
me:
I am my father’s heir and only son.
If I may have your daughter to my wife,
I’ll leave her houses three or four as good,
Within rich Pisa walls, as any one
Old Signior Gremio has in Padua,
Besides two thousand ducats by the year
Of fruitful land, all which shall be her jointure.—
What, have I pinched you, Signior Gremio?

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text:
Two thousand ducats by the year of land!
[aside] My land amounts not to so much in all.—
That she shall have, besides an argosy
That now is lying in Marcellus' road.
[To TRANIO] What, have I choked you with an argosy?

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
[as LUCENTIO] Gremio, ’tis known my father hath no
less
Than three great argosies, besides two galliasses,
And twelve tight galleys. These I will assure her,
And twice as much whate'er thou offer’st next.

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text:
Nay, I have offered all, I have no more,
And she can have no more than all I have.
[To BAPTISTA] If you like me, she shall have me and
mine.

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
[as LUCENTIO]
Why, then the maid is mine from all the world,
By your firm promise. Gremio is outvied.

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text:
I must confess your offer is the best,
And, let your father make her the assurance,
She is your own; else, you must pardon me.
If you should die before him, where’s her dower?

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text: [as LUCENTIO] That’s but a cavil: he is old, I young.

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text: And may not young men die as well as old?

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text:
Well, gentlemen,
I am thus resolved. On Sunday next, you know
My daughter Katherina is to be married.
[To TRANIO as LUCENTIO ]
Now, on the Sunday following, shall Bianca
Be bride to you, if you make this assurance.
If not, to Signior Gremio.
And so I take my leave, and thank you both.

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text: Adieu, good neighbor.
Exit BAPTISTA

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text:
Now I fear thee not.
Sirrah young gamester, your father were a fool
To give thee all and in his waning age
Set foot under thy table. Tut, a toy!
An old Italian fox is not so kind, my boy.
Exit

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
A vengeance on your crafty withered hide!
Yet I have faced it with a card of ten.
'Tis in my head to do my master good.
I see no reason but supposed Lucentio
Must get a father, called “supposed Vincentio”—
And that’s a wonder. Fathers commonly
Do get their children. But in this case of wooing,
A child shall get a sire, if I fail not of my cunning.
Exit
Enter LUCENTIO disguised as CAMBIO, HORTENSIO disguised as LITIO, and BIANCA

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text:
[as CAMBIO] Fiddler, forbear. You grow too forward,
sir.
Have you so soon forgot the entertainment
Her sister Katherina welcomed you withal?

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text:
[as LITIO] But, wrangling pedant, this is
The patroness of heavenly harmony.
Then give me leave to have prerogative,
And when in music we have spent an hour,
Your lecture shall have leisure for as much.

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text:
[as CAMBIO] Preposterous ass, that never read so far
To know the cause why music was ordained.
Was it not to refresh the mind of man
After his studies or his usual pain?
Then give me leave to read philosophy
And, while I pause, serve in your harmony.

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text:
[as LITIO] Sirrah, I will not bear these braves of
thine.

units.speaker: BIANCA
units.text:
Why, gentlemen, you do me double wrong
To strive for that which resteth in my choice.
I am no breeching scholar in the schools.
I’ll not be tied to hours nor 'pointed times
But learn my lessons as I please myself.
And, to cut off all strife, here sit we down.
[To HORTENSIO]
Take you your instrument, play you the whiles.
His lecture will be done ere you have tuned.

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text: [as LITIO] You’ll leave his lecture when I am in tune?

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text:
[aside] That will be never. [To HORTENSIO] Tune your
instrument.

units.speaker: BIANCA
units.text: Where left we last?

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text:
Here, madam:
Hic ibat Simois, hic est Sigeia tellus,
Hic steterat Priami regia celsa senis.

units.speaker: BIANCA
units.text: Construe them.

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text:
Hic ibat , as I told you before, Simois , I am Lucentio,
hic est , son unto Vincentio of Pisa, Sigeia tellus ,
disguised thus to get your love, Hic steterat , and that
“Lucentio” that comes a-wooing, Priami is my man Tranio,
regia , bearing my port, celsa senis, that we might
beguile the old pantaloon.

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text: [as LITIO] Madam, my instrument’s in tune.

units.speaker: BIANCA
units.text: Let’s hear. [he plays] O fie! The treble jars.

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text: [as CAMBIO] Spit in the hole, man, and tune again.

units.speaker: BIANCA
units.text:
Now let me see if I can construe it. Hic ibat Simois ,
I know you not, hic est Sigeia tellus , I trust you not,
Hic steterat Priami , take heed he hear us not, regia ,
presume not, celsa senis, despair not.

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text: [as LITIO] Madam, ’tis now in tune.

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text: [as CAMBIO] All but the base.

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text:
[as LITIO] The base is right; ’tis the base knave
that jars.
[aside] How fiery and forward our pedant is!
Now, for my life, the knave doth court my love.
Pedascule , I’ll watch you better yet.

units.speaker: BIANCA
units.text: [To LUCENTIO] In time I may believe, yet I mistrust.

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text:
Mistrust it not, for sure Aeacides
Was Ajax, called so from his grandfather.

units.speaker: BIANCA
units.text:
I must believe my master; else, I promise you,
I should be arguing still upon that doubt.
But let it rest.—Now, Litio, to you.
Good master, take it not unkindly, pray,
That I have been thus pleasant with you both.

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text:
[as LITIO , to LUCENTIO]
You may go walk, and give me leave awhile.
My lessons make no music in three parts.

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text:
[as CAMBIO] Are you so formal, sir? Well, I must wait.
[aside] And watch withal, for, but I be deceived,
Our fine musician groweth amorous.

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text:
[as LITIO] Madam, before you touch the instrument,
To learn the order of my fingering
I must begin with rudiments of art,
To teach you gamut in a briefer sort,
More pleasant, pithy, and effectual
Than hath been taught by any of my trade.
And there it is in writing, fairly drawn.

units.speaker: BIANCA
units.text: Why, I am past my gamut long ago.

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text: Yet read the gamut of Hortensio.

units.speaker: BIANCA

units.text: Enter a SERVANT

units.speaker: SERVANT
units.text:
Mistress, your father prays you leave your books
And help to dress your sister’s chamber up.
You know tomorrow is the wedding day.

units.speaker: BIANCA
units.text: Farewell, sweet masters both. I must be gone.

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text:
[as CAMBIO] Faith, mistress, then I have no cause to
stay.
Exeunt BIANCA, the SERVANT, and LUCENTIO

units.speaker: HORTENSIO
units.text:
But I have cause to pry into this pedant.
Methinks he looks as though he were in love.
Yet if thy thoughts, Bianca, be so humble
To cast thy wand'ring eyes on every stale,
Seize thee that list! If once I find thee ranging,
Hortensio will be quit with thee by changing.
Exit
Enter BAPTISTA, GREMIO, TRANIO as LUCENTIO, KATHERINE, BIANCA, LUCENTIO, and others, attendants

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text:
[To TRANIO] Signior Lucentio, this is the 'pointed day
That Katherine and Petruchio should be married,
And yet we hear not of our son-in-law.
What will be said? What mockery will it be,
To want the bridegroom when the priest attends
To speak the ceremonial rites of marriage?
What says Lucentio to this shame of ours?

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text:
No shame but mine. I must, forsooth, be forced
To give my hand, opposed against my heart,
Unto a mad-brain rudesby, full of spleen,
Who wooed in haste and means to wed at leisure.
I told you, I, he was a frantic fool,
Hiding his bitter jests in blunt behavior,
And, to be noted for a merry man,
He’ll woo a thousand, 'point the day of marriage,
Make friends, invite, and proclaim the banns,
Yet never means to wed where he hath wooed.
Now must the world point at poor Katherine
And say, “Lo, there is mad Petruchio’s wife,
If it would please him come and marry her!”

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
[as LUCENTIO] Patience, good Katherine, and Baptista
too.
Upon my life, Petruchio means but well,
Whatever fortune stays him from his word:
Though he be blunt, I know him passing wise;
Though he be merry, yet withal he’s honest.

units.speaker: KATHERINE
units.text: Would Katherine had never seen him, though!
Exit weeping, followed by BIANCA and others

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text:
Go, girl. I cannot blame thee now to weep,
For such an injury would vex a very saint,
Much more a shrew of thy impatient humor.
Enter BIONDELLO

units.speaker: BIONDELLO
units.text:
Master, master! News, old news, and such news as you
never heard of!

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text: Is it new and old too? How may that be?

units.speaker: BIONDELLO
units.text: Why, is it not news to hear of Petruchio’s coming?

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text: Is he come?

units.speaker: BIONDELLO
units.text: Why, no, sir.

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text: What then?

units.speaker: BIONDELLO
units.text: He is coming.

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text: When will he be here?

units.speaker: BIONDELLO
units.text: When he stands where I am and sees you there.

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text: [as LUCENTIO] But say, what to thine old news?

units.speaker: BIONDELLO
units.text:
Why, Petruchio is coming in a new hat and an old
jerkin, a pair of old breeches thrice turned, a pair of
boots that have been candle cases, one buckled, another
laced; an old rusty sword ta'en out of the town armory,
with a broken hilt and chapeless; with two broken
points; his horse hipped, with an old mothy saddle and
stirrups of no kindred, besides possessed with the
glanders and like to mose in the chine, troubled with
the lampass, infected with the fashions, full of
wingdalls, sped with spavins, rayed with yellows, past
cure of the fives, stark spoiled with the staggers,
begnawn with the bots, swayed in the back and
shoulder-shotten, near-legged before and with a
half-checked bit and a headstall of sheeps leather,
which, being restrained to keep him from stumbling, hath
been often burst, and now repaired with knots, one
girth six times pieced, and a woman’s crupper of velour,
which hath two letters for her name fairly set down in
studs, and here and there pieced with packthread.

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text: Who comes with him?

units.speaker: BIONDELLO
units.text:
O, sir, his lackey, for all the world caparisoned like
the horse; with a linen stock on one leg and a kersey
boot-hose on the other, gartered with a red and blue
list; an old hat and the humor of forty fancies pricked
in ’t for a feather. A monster, a very monster in
apparel, and not like a Christian footboy or a
gentleman’s lackey.

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
[as LUCENTIO]
'Tis some odd humor pricks him to this fashion,
Yet oftentimes he goes but mean-appareled.

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text: I am glad he’s come, howsoe'er he comes.

units.speaker: BIONDELLO
units.text: Why, sir, he comes not.

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text: Didst thou not say he comes?

units.speaker: BIONDELLO
units.text: Who? That Petruchio came?

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text: Ay, that Petruchio came.

units.speaker: BIONDELLO
units.text: No, sir, I say his horse comes, with him on his back.

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text: Why, that’s all one.

units.speaker: BIONDELLO
units.text:
Nay, by Saint Jamy,
I hold you a penny,
A horse and a man
Is more than one
And yet not many.
Enter PETRUCHIO and GRUMIO

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: Come, where be these gallants? Who’s at home?

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text: You are welcome, sir.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: And yet I come not well.

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text: And yet you halt not.

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text: [as LUCENTIO] Not so well appareled as I wish you were.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
Were it better I should rush in thus—
But where is Kate? Where is my love?
How does my father? Gentles, methinks you frown.
And wherefore gaze this goodly company
As if they saw some wondrous monument,
Some comet or unusual prodigy?

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text:
Why, sir, you know this is your wedding day.
First were we sad, fearing you would not come,
Now sadder that you come so unprovided.
Fie, doff this habit, shame to your estate,
An eyesore to our solemn festival.

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
And tell us what occasion of import
Hath all so long detained you from your wife
And sent you hither so unlike yourself.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
Tedious it were to tell and harsh to hear.
Sufficeth I am come to keep my word,
Though in some part enforcèd to digress,
Which, at more leisure, I will so excuse
As you shall well be satisfied withal.
But where is Kate? I stay too long from her.
The morning wears. 'Tis time we were at church.

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
See not your bride in these unreverent robes.
Go to my chamber, put on clothes of mine.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text: Not I, believe me. Thus I’ll visit her.

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text: But thus, I trust, you will not marry her.

units.speaker: PETRUCHIO
units.text:
Good sooth, even thus. Therefore, ha' done with words:
To me she’s married, not unto my clothes.
Could I repair what she will wear in me
As I can change these poor accoutrements,
'Twere well for Kate and better for myself.
But what a fool am I to chat with you,
When I should bid good morrow to my bride
And seal the title with a lovely kiss!
Exeunt PETRUCHIO and GRUMIO

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
He hath some meaning in his mad attire.
We will persuade him, be it possible,
To put on better ere he go to church.

units.speaker: BAPTISTA
units.text: I’ll after him, and see the event of this.
Exeunt BAPTISTA, GREMIO, and attendants

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
But sir, to love concerneth us to add
Her father’s liking, which to bring to pass,
As I before unparted to your worship,
I am to get a man—whate'er he be
It skills not much, we’ll fit him to our turn—
And he shall be “Vincentio of Pisa”
And make assurance here in Padua
Of greater sums than I have promisèd.
So shall you quietly enjoy your hope
And marry sweet Bianca with consent.

units.speaker: LUCENTIO
units.text:
Were it not that my fellow schoolmaster
Doth watch Bianca’s steps so narrowly,
'Twere good, methinks, to steal our marriage,
Which, once performed, let all the world say no,
I’ll keep mine own despite of all the world.

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
That by degrees we mean to look into
And watch our vantage in this business.
We’ll overreach the graybeard, Gremio,
The narrow-prying father, Minola,
The quaint musician, amorous Litio,
All for my master’s sake, Lucentio.
Enter GREMIO

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text: Signior Gremio, came you from the church?

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text: As willingly as e'er I came from school.

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text:
[as LUCENTIO] And is the bride and bridegroom coming
home?

units.speaker: GREMIO
units.text:
A bridegroom, say you? 'Tis a groom indeed,
A grumbling groom, and that the girl shall find.

units.speaker: TRANIO
units.text: [as LUCENTIO] Curster than she? Why, ’tis impossible.

units.speaker: GREMIO
